title: SEGMENT 3 — The trophoblast missing piece: Niakan, Okae, and the second stem cell

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — The trophoblast missing piece: Niakan, Okae, and the second stem cell
Let me back up to a piece of history that, until quite recently, was the rate-limiting step for the whole field.
Embryonic stem cells were derived from mouse blastocysts in 1981 — Martin Evans and Matthew Kaufman in Cambridge, and independently Gail Martin at UCSF.

They cracked the recipe for taking the inner cell mass out of a blastocyst, plating it in the right conditions, and getting cells that would self-renew indefinitely while retaining the ability to become any tissue.
That was a Nobel-prize-winning discovery and it transformed biology.
Human embryonic stem cells followed in 1998 — Jamie Thomson at Wisconsin.
iPSCs followed in 2006 — Yamanaka at Kyoto.
By the late 2000s, we had a robust toolkit for working with the inner cell mass lineage.
We could grow it, manipulate it, freeze it, differentiate it down any number of developmental paths.
But here's what we couldn't do.
We couldn't reliably grow trophoblast stem cells — at least not from humans.
Trophoblast, remember, is the other lineage — the outer layer of the blastocyst that becomes the placenta.
If you want to build a structure that looks like a complete blastocyst, you need both lineages: pluripotent cells for the inner part, trophoblast cells for the outer part.

With only embryonic stem cells, you could make pluripotent aggregates — embryoid bodies — that did a lot of interesting differentiation but never organized into anything resembling a real blastocyst, because they didn't have the outer-layer cells.
In 1998, the same year Thomson derived human embryonic stem cells, a research group led by Janet Rossant in Toronto derived mouse trophoblast stem cells.

They took cells from a particular region of the mouse blastocyst — the polar trophectoderm — and showed that with the right culture conditions, including high levels of FGF4 and serum, those cells could be grown as stable, self-renewing trophoblast cells.
They retained the ability to differentiate into the various trophoblast lineages that make up the placenta.
Mouse trophoblast stem cells became, from then on, a standard reagent.
But humans were different.
For nearly two decades after the mouse work, nobody could derive stable human trophoblast stem cells.
People tried, repeatedly.
The mouse recipe didn't work on human cells.
Human trophoblast, in culture, would either die, or stop being trophoblast, or terminally differentiate.
You couldn't get a stable line.
That changed in two stages, both quite recent.
Kathy Niakan, working at the Francis Crick Institute in London, and her colleagues, around 2014, started doing careful single-cell transcriptomics on human blastocysts.
They were trying to understand which signaling pathways were actually active in human trophectoderm, and how those differed from what was active in mouse trophectoderm.
They found important differences.
The signaling logic of human early development isn't a perfect copy of mouse.
Human trophoblast, for instance, depends much less on FGF4 than mouse trophoblast does.
Other pathways — particularly TGF-beta family signaling, Wnt signaling, hippo pathway components — turned out to be more important than people had assumed.
This was the conceptual groundwork.
It told us why the mouse recipe didn't translate, and it pointed at what the human recipe should contain.
Hiroaki Okae, working in Takahiro Arima's group at Tohoku University in Japan, took that conceptual groundwork and turned it into a working protocol.
In 2018, Okae and colleagues published in Cell Stem Cell a recipe for deriving human trophoblast stem cells from blastocysts and from first-trimester placental tissue.

The key ingredients turned out to be a Wnt activator (CHIR99021), an EGF source, several inhibitors of TGF-beta and other differentiation-pushing signals (A83-01, SB431542, Y-27632), and the right combination of substrate and basal medium.
With this cocktail, human trophoblast cells finally became cultivable as a stable, self-renewing stem cell population.
They could be grown indefinitely.
They could be differentiated into the major trophoblast subtypes — extravillous trophoblast, syncytiotrophoblast, and so on — that you find in a real placenta.
That's the breakthrough.
For a long time we had embryonic stem cells but couldn't model the trophoblast.
Niakan and Okae finally derived stable trophoblast stem cells.
We now had the second ingredient.
The recipe was complete.
I want you to feel why this matters.

With only embryonic or pluripotent stem cells, the best you could do was an embryoid body — a clump of cells that differentiates randomly into pieces of various tissues, in no particular order or arrangement.
That's useful, but it's not an embryo.
A blastocyst has two cooperating populations.
Trophoblast on the outside, signaling inward.
Inner cell mass on the inside, signaling outward.
The two are talking to each other constantly through Wnt, BMP, Nodal — the embryo is a conversation.
Without trophoblast stem cells, you have only half of that conversation.
With trophoblast stem cells, suddenly you can recreate the conversation, in a dish, between two real, separately derived, propagatable cell populations.
This is what made blastoids possible.
Within months of Okae's paper, multiple labs were racing to combine human pluripotent cells with human trophoblast cells and see what happened.
And what happened, as we'll see in a minute, was remarkable.
There's a third population worth mentioning here too: extraembryonic endoderm, sometimes called primitive endoderm or hypoblast.
In the real embryo, this is a thin layer of cells that emerges from the inner cell mass and goes on to form the yolk sac.
For a complete model of post-implantation development, you also need this lineage.
Recipes for primitive endoderm-like stem cells were developed in parallel — in mice, by various groups in the 2010s; in humans, more recently.
These cells are often called XEN cells (for extraembryonic endoderm).
They're the third ingredient.
With embryonic, trophoblast, and XEN cells in hand, you have all the major lineages of the early embryo as separately culturable stem cell populations.
And that, as we'll see in the next segment, is what enabled the ETX embryoids.
